stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[d1]] & 0 & 0 \\ [[l21]] & [[d2]] & 0 \\ [[l31]] & [[l32]] & [[d3]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is the smallest eigenvalue of \( A \)?
solution: The matrix is lower triangular, so its eigenvalues are exactly the diagonal entries \( [[d1]], [[d2]], [[d3]] \). The smallest of these is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is one of the diagonal entries, but not the smallest eigenvalue.
This is one of the diagonal entries, but not the smallest eigenvalue.
This is one of the diagonal entries, but not the smallest eigenvalue.
This is the trace (sum of the diagonal), not the smallest eigenvalue.
This is one more than the correct smallest eigenvalue.
This is four more than the correct smallest eigenvalue.
This is three less than the correct smallest eigenvalue.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[d1]] & [[u12]] & [[u13]] \\ 0 & [[d2]] & [[u23]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[d3]] \end{bmatrix} \), an upper triangular matrix. What is the product of all three of its eigenvalues?
solution: For a triangular matrix, the eigenvalues are exactly the diagonal entries \( [[d1]], [[d2]], [[d3]] \), so the product of all the eigenvalues is \( [[d1]]\cdot[[d2]]\cdot[[d3]] = [[answer]] \) (this also equals \( \det(A) \), since the off-diagonal entries never affect it).
distractor_rationales: This sums the diagonal entries (the trace) instead of multiplying them.
This sums the pairwise products of the diagonal entries instead of the full triple product.
This incorrectly drags in an off-diagonal entry, which never affects the eigenvalues of a triangular matrix.
This incorrectly drags in an off-diagonal entry, which never affects the eigenvalues of a triangular matrix.
This is one more than the correct product.
This is one less than the correct product.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[d1]] & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & [[d2]] & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & [[d3]] \end{bmatrix} \), a diagonal matrix. What is the sum of all three of its eigenvalues?
solution: For a diagonal matrix, the eigenvalues are precisely the diagonal entries themselves, \( [[d1]], [[d2]], [[d3]] \), so their sum is \( [[d1]] + [[d2]] + [[d3]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the diagonal entries (their product) instead of adding them.
This only uses one of the three diagonal entries.
This omits the third diagonal entry from the sum.
This mixes a product of two entries with the third instead of summing all three.
This is one more than the correct sum.
This is two less than the correct sum.

stem: Let \( A(t) = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]]t + ([[b]]) & [[u12]] & [[u13]] \\ 0 & [[c]] & [[u23]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[e]] \end{bmatrix} \). For what value of \( t \) does \( A(t) \) have \( 0 \) as an eigenvalue?
solution: \( A(t) \) is upper triangular, so its eigenvalues are exactly its diagonal entries \( [[a]]t+([[b]]), [[c]], [[e]] \). Since \( [[c]] \) and \( [[e]] \) are never \( 0 \), \( 0 \) is an eigenvalue exactly when \( [[a]]t + ([[b]]) = 0 \), i.e. \( t = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign inside the fraction from the correct solution of \( at+b=0 \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This inverts the ratio \( b/a \) instead of solving \( at+b=0 \) for \( t \).
This inverts the ratio and flips the sign.
This is one more than the correct value of \( t \).
This is one less than the correct value of \( t \).

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[d1]] & 0 & [[x13]] & [[x14]] \\ 0 & [[d2]] & [[x23]] & [[x24]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[s]] & [[w]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[w]] & [[s]] \end{bmatrix} \), a block upper triangular matrix with diagonal blocks \( \begin{bmatrix} [[d1]] & 0 \\ 0 & [[d2]] \end{bmatrix} \) and \( \begin{bmatrix} [[s]] & [[w]] \\ [[w]] & [[s]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is the largest eigenvalue of \( A \)?
solution: For a block upper triangular matrix, the full set of eigenvalues is the union of the eigenvalues of its diagonal blocks. The top-left block contributes \( [[d1]] \) and \( [[d2]] \); the bottom-right symmetric block contributes \( [[s]]+[[w]] \) and \( [[s]]-[[w]] \). The largest of all four is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is one eigenvalue from the top-left block, but not necessarily the overall largest.
This is one eigenvalue from the top-left block, but not necessarily the overall largest.
This is one eigenvalue from the bottom-right block, but not necessarily the overall largest.
This is the other eigenvalue from the bottom-right block, but not necessarily the overall largest.
This is one more than the correct largest eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct largest eigenvalue.
